1998-12-01.title: THE MASKING OF A MILITANT: A special report.; A Soldier's Shadowy Trail In U.S. and in the Mideast
1998-12-01.abstract: Special report, Masking of a Militant, on shadowy past of Ali A Mohamed, former Egyptian Army officer who has been jailed in New York on charges of conspiring with Osama bin Laden, Saudi exile sought in US embassy bombings in Africa; Mohamed, who was turned down for job as CIA operative in 1984, entered US legally in 1985 despite being on State Department's terrorist 'watch list'; subsequently cultivated range of useful relationships with American Govt, such as enlisting in Army and being cooperating witness for FBI; his aggressive support for Islamic causes and open curiosity about intelligence matters raised eyebrows among colleagues in Army's elite Special Forces unit; before honorable discharge he regularly traveled to New York City area to train in basic military doctrine circle of Islamic militants, many of whom have since been convicted of plotting series of terrorist attacks, including 1993 bombing of World Trade Center; Mohamed reportedly forged ties with bin Laden as early as 1991; was said to be adept at obtaining false documents for bin Laden's organization and to have assisted with logistical tasks, such as bin Laden's move from Afghanistan to Sudan in 1991; US officials say he was fully enmeshed in dual life in early 1990's, simultaneously collaborating with bin Laden while talking regularly with Federal agents about Saudi exile and other issues; say case is modern espionage parable in which Mohamed was trying to manipulate American investigators even as they were using him to gain window into bin Laden's activities; photos (L)

1998-12-01.title: Air of Crisis in Zimbabwe Grows After Threat to Seize 841 Farms
1998-12-01.abstract: Threat by Zimbabwe's Government to seize 841 farms, owned mostly by whites, has thrown commercial farmers into confusion and added to country's sense of crisis; whites, fewer than 2 percent of population, own 70 percent of land, while millions of rural blacks are crowded onto 'communal areas' of stony depleted soil (M)

1998-12-01.title: U.S. Pledges Military Cooperation to Colombia in Drug War
1998-12-01.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen and Colombia's new President, Andres Pastrana, announce steps to intensify military cooperation in war on drug trafficking, including pledge to increase Pentagon training of Colombia's armed forces and share more aerial and satellite intelligence data; both countries will sign agreement setting up formal working group to bring together officials from both countries for regular consultations; agreement follows sharp increase in aid and equipment, including six Black Hawk helicopters that Congress approved for fight against narcotics as part of recent increase in defense spending; Cohen's aides insist that new help to Colombia's police and military will only involve fight against drug treaffickers and not against leftist insurgents (M)

1998-12-01.title: U.S. and Other Nations Plan More Aid for Palestinians
1998-12-01.abstract: US pledges extra $400 million aid to Palestinians, which is expected to nearly double amount of their economic assistance from US over next five years; announcement is made by Pres Clinton at start of one-day international donor conference in Washington sponsored by State Dept, which brought estimated total of more than $3 billion in new pledges; Clinton says US decided to increase aid in hopes of convincing Palestinians to stick to peace accord with Israel; appeals for generous contributions from other wealthy nations, adding that peace efforts have so far brought little tangible improvements in lives of Palestinians; Yasir Arafat meets with Clinton at White House and warns donor nations that Palestinian economy has experienced continuous decline; accuses Israel of undermining economy through repeated closings of common border in response to alleged terrorist threats; photo (M)

1998-12-01.title: Mexico City Journal; Above the Law: Bodyguards Swell a Wave of Crime
1998-12-01.abstract: Legions of revolver-waving bodyguards speeding in unmarked cars to protect limousines are newest danger for motorists on mean streets of Mexico City and are complicating country's efforts to subdue tidal wave of crime; private security guards, mostly armed but untrained, seem to rule streets in affluent neighborhoods, and authorities acknowledge they have little control over them; their abuses against unsuspecting passers-by grow more frequent each day--as are violent crimes by crooks posing as private policemen; chart photo (M)

1998-12-02.title: Quebec Questions
1998-12-02.abstract: Anthony DePalma analysis contends that while result of Quebec's provincial election are muddy enough for both major parties to claim victory of sorts, and to bear some degree of defeat, real losers are those who had hoped election would provide clear sense of where Quebec, and Canada, are heading; notes that while separatists were returned to power, they could not even muster plurality of popular vote; holds that election results keep independence movement alive, but it is wounded; says that means Premier Lucien Bouchard will not call another referendum on breaking away from Canada anytime soon (M)

1998-12-02.title: Risk of Social Crisis Rising In Asia, UN Agency Finds
1998-12-02.abstract: HONG KONG—  While some upbeat forecasters are saying the Asian economic crisis has bottomed out, an agency of the United Nations said Tuesday that the region's social crisis was far from over and was likely to get worse.

1998-12-02.title: Battle Looms in Israel Over 'Mixed' Religious Councils
1998-12-02.abstract: Israeli Goverment, despite numerous appeals, may have to integrate its powerful all-Orthodox religious councils with Reform and Conservative Jews, in keeping with Supreme Court ruling that has outraged Ultra-Orthodox politicians; they have threatened to bring down Government if councils become 'mixed'; photo (M)

1998-12-02.title: U.S. Will Sell New Zealand Planes It Denied to Pakistan
1998-12-02.abstract: US has finally found customer for fighter planes that it sold to Pakistan but never delivered; New Zealand has agreed to acquire 28 F-16 fighters on 10-year lease-buy arrangement for about $105 million (M)

1998-12-02.title: Albright Speaks With Emotion at Holocaust Conference
1998-12-02.abstract: Sec of State Madeleine K Albright, speaking at Washington, DC, conference on Holocaust-era assets, talks emotionally about Jewish roots that she acknowledged almost two years ago and her own loss of family members whom she never knew; photo (M)

1998-12-03.title: Decisions by U.S. and I.M.F. Worsened Asia's Problems, the World Bank Finds
1998-12-03.abstract: World Bank report concludes that decision last year by International Monetary Fund and US Treasury to push Asian nations to send their interest rates soaring was crucial blunder that worsened world financial crisis; graph (M)

1998-12-03.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1998-12-03.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: TALKS ON ECONOMIC CRISIS -- The head of the International Monetary Fund, Michel Camdessus, met again with Prime Minister Yevgeny M. Primakov and praised Russia's ''pragmatic'' course in dealing with its economic crisis. But Mr. Camdessus said a team from the Fund would have to return next month for further negotiations on releasing the frozen loans that Russia  badly needs.   (AP)    RUSSIA: STATUE TO RISE AGAIN -- The Communist- dominated lower house voted overwhelmingly to resurrect a statue of Felix Dzerzhinsky, left, the first chief of the Soviet Union's secret police, in the center of Moscow. The towering bronze statue of ''Iron Felix,'' a landmark during the Soviet era, was toppled by pro-democracy protesters in 1991 and has since languished in a city park.   (AP)

1998-12-03.title: Senior Aide Implicating Bin Laden In Terrorism
1998-12-03.abstract: Government officials say unidentified senior aide of exiled Saudi terrorist Osama bin Laden has been talking to US investigators for two years; say informer pleaded guilty to terrorism in secret proceeding in Federal court, Manhattan; at least one operative of bin Laden's group in East Africa concluded over year ago that informer was Sidi Tayyib, businessman who was married to one of bin Laden's relatives (M)

1998-12-03.title: Mexico's Oil Monopoly Accused Of Polluting Scores of Gulf Sites
1998-12-03.abstract: Environmental group Greenpeace accuses Petroleos Mexicanos, state-owned petroleum company known as Pemex, of dumping vast amounts of toxic waste into scores of illegal sites on the Gulf Coast; Greenpeace dir Alejandro Calvillo says Pemex is main environmental criminal in Mexico (M)

1998-12-03.title: The Latin Arms Explosion That Fizzled
1998-12-03.abstract: Wave of arms sales that many officials predicted after Clinton Administration lifted de facto ban on sales of most advanced American weapons to Latin American countries 16 months ago has not materialized, and there are few signs that any country will be able to afford weapons (M)

1998-12-04.title: Ulster Negotiations Stall Again, Unraveling This Time on Details
1998-12-04.abstract: Protestant and Roman Catholic leader apparently fail to agree on several important issues in Northern Ireland peace effort (S)

1998-12-04.title: For Israeli Soldiers, Pride and Dread in Lebanon
1998-12-04.abstract: Israeli soldiers serving in Lebanon feel both pride and dread, a consequence of sporadically deadly conflict between Israelis and Shiite Muslim guerrillas who bomb and run; seven Israelis have been killed in two weeks; photo; map (M)

1998-12-04.title: Election Set in Indonesia but Protests Persist
1998-12-04.abstract: Indonesian Govt sets June 7 as date for national elections for new House of Representatives, and say it will convene People's Consultative Assembly on Aug 29 to elect new Pres; students who have been pressing Pres B J Habibie for swifter democratic reforms continue their protests; block Jakarta's main thoroughfare, demnanding arrest of Suharto, former Pres, on corruption charges (S)

1998-12-04.title: World Briefing
1998-12-04.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: TORY REBELLION -- William Hague, left, the Conservative Party leader, found himself at odds with one group he thought he could count on, the Tory peers in the House of Lords. Mr. Hague dismissed his party's leader in the House of Lords, Viscount Cranborne, for striking a deal that could let 10 percent of the 759 hereditary peers retain their voting rights. Mr. Hague later endorsed the same legislative proposal himself, prompting the entire Tory leadership of the Lords to resign their front bench posts. Warren Hoge (NYT)

1998-12-04.title: Fugitive Zimbabwe Ex-President Has Odd Meeting With Mandela
1998-12-04.abstract: South Africa Pres Nelson Mandela, in move that outrages opposition parties and baffles police and foreign affairs officials, meets quietly with Rev Canaan Banana, former Pres of Zimbabwe, who is a fugitive after being convicted last week in Zimbabwe of sodomy and assault (S)

1998-12-05.title: Symbols Cast Shadows On Clinton Trip to Gaza
1998-12-05.abstract: Issue of Palestinian statehood complicates Pres Clinton's trip to Israel and Palestinian Authority, and details of trip, such as the aircraft he uses and the sites he visits, will be scrutinized for their symbolic bearing on the issue (M)

1998-12-05.title: Monk Factions Vie to Control Korea's Biggest Sect : Buddhist Temple Tug-of-War
1998-12-05.abstract: SEOUL—  Inside a basement tearoom secluded from the wreckage of nights of mayhem outside Korea's leading Buddhist temple, Mu Hyu boasted Friday of his ability to survive against the "gangsters" he said were trying to regain control of the order for personal profit and power.

1998-12-05.title: 2 Mexican Parties Near Accord on Bank Bailout
1998-12-05.abstract: Agreement on measures to refinance Mexican Government's troubled $61 billion bank bailout is reach by members of Congress from Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI) and National Action Party, but the latter demands that Guillermo Ortiz resign as head of Bank of Mexico as part of agreement; PRI, following cues from Pres Ernesto Zedillo, opposes Ortiz's resignation on ground that central bank enjoys independence (M)

1998-12-05.title: In a Key Test, Nigerians Go to Polls
1998-12-05.abstract: Tens of millions of Nigerians are expected to vote in local government elections on Saturday in the first of three elections that the military rulers have promised will lead to democracy.    The borders will be closed during the voting hours, officials said, to prevent candidates from paying foreigners to vote illegally. In Lagos, the largest city, the authorities announced that most cars would be banned from the streets and that  pedestrians would be limited to areas near voting centers.

1998-12-05.title: Suharto Says His Millions Were Earned, Not Stolen
1998-12-05.abstract: Suharto, former President of Indonesia, defends himself against charges of corruptly amassing huge personal fortune; says he is a good saver and landlord; estimates of his wealth range up to $40 billion; photo of demostrators calling for broader investigations of corruption under Suharto (M)

1998-12-06.title: Two Attacks Cause Worry To the Critics Of Iran Rulers
1998-12-06.abstract: Critics of Iran's conservative government and foreign diplomats are alarmed by the attack on 13 visiting American businessmen on Nov 21 and the fatal stabbing of Dariush Foruhar, a prominent dissident, and his wife, Parveneh, a day later; suspect the attacks may have been staged with at least tacit government approval; Pres Mohammed Khatami, a relative moderate, has condemned attacks in strong terms, but conservative figures who still wield most power in Iran have expressed fewer regrets (M)

1998-12-06.title: Squabbling, Spaniards Mark 20th Birthday of Their Constitution
1998-12-06.abstract: Spain marks 20th anniversary of its Constitution amid squabbling over whether it should be rewritten to shift more power to regions; drive for greater regional autonomy is led by Basques but is joined also by nationalists in Catalonia and Galicia (M)

1998-12-06.title: North Korean Nuclear Arms Pact Reported Near Breakdown
1998-12-06.abstract: North Korea refuses to allow inspection of huge underground construction site that could be used to hide a nuclear reactor, a stand that could cause collapse of four-year-old agreement meant to freeze North Korea's nuclear weapons program; American officials are also alarmed by intelligence evidence suggesting that North Korea is making preparations to test-fire a medium-range ballistic missile over Japan (M)

1998-12-06.title: New Weapons Sales to Africa Trouble Arms-Control Experts
1998-12-06.abstract: The United States and 32 other countries concluded four days of arms control meetings here with a public commitment to exercise ''maximum restraint'' before selling weapons to regions of conflict.    Behind closed doors, the delegates to the so-called Wassenaar regime, an arms control organization that came into being after the cold war, made clear that the areas they were most concerned about were Central Africa, where a half dozen countries are fighting in Congo, and the Horn of Africa, where Ethiopia and Eritrea are on the brink of full-scale war.

1998-12-06.title: Last Rebels In Cambodia Send Message Of Surrender
1998-12-06.abstract: Several hundred Khmer Rouge guerrillas surrender to Government in what is presented as final end to Cambodia's armed Communist movement; Ta Mok, Khieu Samphan and Nuon Chea, three top Khmer Rouge leaders who are targets of possible international tribunal for crimes against humanity, do not join in ceremony and remain at large (M)

1998-12-07.title: Beijing Journal; Please, Mr. Bureaucrat, Hear My 20-Year-Old Plea
1998-12-07.abstract: Hundreds of elderly in Beijing, China, are carrying on ancient traditon of appealing personally to higher authorities for redress of heartfelt grievances--some waiting for decades, camped outside city's Petitions and Appeals Office; many sleep in shoddy tents of canvas or plastic, cook food scrounged from garbage and use streets or nearby canal as toilet; some grievances date back to Cultural Revolution of 1966 to 1976; direct appeal to higher authorities was main means of redress when country lacked formal legal system; after Communists came to power in 1949, tradition was continued with formal appeals offices in provinces, and in Beijing for those still not satisfied; photos (M)

1998-12-07.title: Angry Ex-Ally Rejects Netanyahu's Call to Rejoin the Cabinet
1998-12-07.abstract: David Levy, Israel's former Foreign Minister, says he will not rejoin Israeli Cabinet, potentially foiling Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu's latest effort to hold together his right-wing coalition since he signed land-for security accord with Palestinians in October; Levy heads five-member Gesher parliamentary group (M)

1998-12-07.title: As Most of Ireland Prospers, the Poor Lament a Deepening Social Chasm
1998-12-07.abstract: Gap between poor and well-to-do in Ireland is apparently widening even though country's economic boom of past several years has made it 19th richest country in world with fastest growth rate of any European Union nation; educated professionals and middle class administrative and technical workers are thriving in places such as Dublin, in contrast to those in poor areas of city, where unemployment averages 40 percent--with pockets where 90 percent have been out of work, some for several years; major problem keeping many from profiting from boom is that about 40 percent of men over 35 are functionally illiterate, descendants of dock workers whose jobs disappeared 25 years ago with arrival of huge containerships; photos (M)

1998-12-07.title: Rise in Cigarette Smoking Doesn't Bother Burma Government
1998-12-07.abstract: MANDALAY, Burma—  When U Soe Thein Oo attended a concert a few months ago by Iron Cross, the most popular Burmese heavy metal band, he got more than just an earful of "Desert Moon," the band's hit love ballad.

1998-12-07.title: Monitors Call Nigerian Vote Fair, With Room to Improve
1998-12-07.abstract: Officials who monitored Nigeria's local Government elections say, despite widespread logistical problems, voting was fair and is positive step toward presidential elections in February 1999; photo (M)

1998-12-08.title: Pushing the Limits Of an Irreplaceable Resource
1998-12-08.abstract: William K Stevens article surveys man's effects on the earth's water cycle and progress toward mitigating some of the harm he has done; photos; map; graph (M)

1998-12-08.title: Dollars Are Weapon of Choice in the War on Bacteria Peril
1998-12-08.abstract: Article describes US efforts to expand cooperation between Russian and American biologists as part of effort to stem brain drain, discover more about Soviet germ warfare arsenal and learn from Russian strides in germ defenses; US will spend up to $6 million to upgrade security at Russia's germ bank that holds virus collection; has persuaded Europe and Japan to join it in financing International Science and Technology Center in Moscow, which tries to find peaceful work for former weapons scientists (M)

1998-12-08.title: China Appears Ready for Trial Of a Leader of Outlawed Party
1998-12-08.abstract: Wang Youcai, founder of China's suppressed Democracy Party, will be tried on December 17 on charge of colluding with foreign enemies to incite subversion of state power (S)

1998-12-08.title: World Briefing
1998-12-08.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: FINANCIAL WOES PERSIST  The arrival of a World Bank team for more talks about loans did little to assuage fears that Russia will be left without foreign aid and may begin printing large sums of money to pay off a total foreign currency debt of $158.8 billion by January.    (AP)    BRITAIN: TORY BACKS DOWN ON KEY PLAN  William Hague, the leader of Britain's Conservatives, has abandoned plans to press for a new regional Parliament to represent England. The proposal, a centerpiece of his program at the annual party convention two months ago, was aimed at being a counterweight to the new Parliaments that the Labor Government of Prime Minister Tony Blair has created in Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales.    Warren Hoge (NYT)    GERMANY: CALL FOR EAST GERMAN AMNESTY  Leaders of the Party of Democratic Socialism, successor to the Communists who governed the former East Germany, have called for an amnesty on crimes committed under Communist rule. The party's leader, Lothar Bisby, questioned whether people carrying out the law of East Germany, from border guards upward, should be liable to prosecution. Nearly 1,000 people were killed trying to flee to the West.    (Reuters)    MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: KHATAMI SPEAKS OUT ON REFORMS  President Mohammad Khatami, who was elected last year on promises of ushering in political reforms, accused conservative and traditionalist forces of blocking reforms in Iran. ''We are facing the three currents of counter-revolution, traditionalists hostile to progressive religion and revisionists,'' he told an assembly hall packed with 10,000 student supporters at Sharif University, Teheran.    (Agence France-Presse)    EGYPT: MOVE ON MINI-MOSQUES  The Ministry of Religious Endowments said it began putting into action a plan to control 6,000 ''zawya,'' or mini-mosques, in an attempt to put an end to Muslim fundamentalism. The ministry will appoint prayer leaders to the mosques, a spokesman said.    (Agence France-Presse)    ASIA    INDIA: STANDING FIRM ON KASHMIR  On his first visit to Kashmir since becoming Prime Minister of India in March, Atal Behari Vajpayee said India would never relinquish any of it to Pakistan.   Celia W. Dugger (NYT)    CHINA: ACID RAIN DAMAGE GROWS  China's rapid economic growth and reliance on coal have spread acid rain damage to 40 percent of the country, The China Daily reported. According to a new report by Chinese scientists, China emitted 23.5 million tons of sulfur dioxide last year, up 30 percent from 1993, and coal use doubled to comprise 75 percent of total energy consumption.    (AP)    NORTH KOREA: TALKS WITH U.S. CONTINUE  The United States and North Korea resumed talks in Washington on an American demand for access to an underground building site that could be connected with a revived nuclear program in North Korea. The negotiators met in New York on Friday and Saturday, with their agenda restricted to Washington's insistence that North Korea allow inspections of the site at the village of Kumchang-ri.    (Reuters)    JAPAN: ECONOMY IN 'SEVERE STATE'  The head of the Bank of Japan said Japan's economy remains in ''an extremely severe state.'' The central bank governor, Masaru Hayami, told Parliament that corporate earnings had slipped and industrial production had fallen.    (AP)    AFRICA    SOUTH AFRICA: ELECTION PLAN CHALLENGED  The opposition National Party said it would ask the High Court to rule on the constitutionality of requiring citizens to have new ''bar-coded'' identity documents before they can register to vote in national elections next year. The ruling African National Congress is insisting on the documents, but that could result in fewer white voters getting to the polls because many have not applied for the new documents.    Suzanne Daley (NYT)    UGANDA: PRESIDENT'S BROTHER QUITS  The brother of Uganda's President resigned as the nation's security adviser after admitting his involvement in a secret deal to buy a 49 percent interest in the Uganda Commercial Bank. Maj. Gen. Salim Saleh, a military adviser to his brother, President Yoweri Museveni, has been criticized for corruption and the military's support of the rebellion in Congo.    Ian Fisher (NYT)    ANGOLA: REBELS TELL OF ATTACKS  The rebel movement Unita said Government forces bombed its strongholds in the towns of Andulo and Bailundo in breach of a four-year peace treaty. Paulo Lukamba Gato, Unita's secretary general, told a Portuguese radio station that the attacks could signal a return to all-out fighting in Angola.    (Reuters)    THE AMERICAS    ST. VINCENT AND THE GRENADINES: MARIJUANA WAR  Caribbean troops backed by six United States Marine Corps helicopters are beginning a two-week campaign to eradicate an estimated 12,000 acres of marijuana on St. Vincent, BBC News reported. It said the island's marijuana growers' association, warning that the destruction of the crop would drive up the island's 40 percent unemployment rate, has written to President Clinton demanding compensation.    UNITED STATES: GORE'S GLOBAL CONFERENCE  Vice President Gore is inviting law enforcement and other officials from more than 60 countries to what his office calls the world's first conference on fighting corruption and safeguarding integrity among justice and security officials. The conference is scheduled from Feb. 24 to 26 at the State Department.   (NYT)     Compiled by Christopher S. Wren

1998-12-08.title: Schroder Tries For Consensus In Fight to Cut Jobless Rate
1998-12-08.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroeder, seeking consensus on ways of combating cripplingly high unemployment of 10.6 percent, summons labor unions, employers and several members of Cabinet to first meeting of much-vaunted 'alliance for jobs' but initial achievement is little more than list of good intentions; as global competition has increased while wage costs have remained high country's economy, based on free market and high level of social protection, has frayed; Schroeder's chosen method of building consensus is seen as attempt to demonstrate that German model that has had great difficulty responding to challenges of 1990's can be revived (M)

1998-12-09.title: In Iran City, Prophet's Day Is Safe Excuse For a Party
1998-12-09.abstract: Tabriz, Iran, celebrates 1,129th birthday of prophet Mahdi, whom Shiite Muslims believe will return someday; photo (M)

1998-12-09.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1998-12-09.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: START II DEBATE POSTPONED -- The Parliament again postponed discussion of Start II, the strategic-arms reduction treaty that the United States and Russia signed in 1993. The Duma Council of the Parliament delayed debate because only the liberal Yabloko Party had submitted proposals, the Interfax news agency reported. Yabloko leaders accused Communist legislators of stalling on the treaty, which the United States Senate ratified in 1996.   (AP)    BRITAIN: PINOCHET LAWYERS SEE BIAS -- Lawyers for Gen.  Augusto Pinochet, the former Chilean dictator, questioned the impartiality of a decision on Nov. 25 by the Law Lords, England's highest court, denying the general immunity from arrest on charges of human rights violations. The lawyers complained that a judge, Lord Hoffmann, left, was a director of a fund-raising charity associated with Amnesty International, which joined the case against the general. A spokeswoman for Amnesty International said Lord Hoffmann was an unpaid director of the charity, but was not directly involved with Amnesty International.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

1998-12-09.title: Europe Looks Quizzically at U.S. Proposal for NATO Strategy
1998-12-09.abstract: US proposals for broad new strategic concept for NATO, to counter global threat from chemical, nuclear and biological weapons, receive quizzical responses from European allies at NATO conference in Brussels; many allies resist Sec of State Madeleine K Albright's suggestion that alliance could intervene in regional crises even if it did not have explicit authorization from United Nations (M)

1998-12-09.title: Unicef Study Predicts 16% World Illiteracy Rate Will Increase
1998-12-09.abstract: Unicef study finds that nearly one-sixth of 5.9 billion people in world cannot read or write and predicts that illiteracy rates will steadily grow into next century (M)

1998-12-09.title: Satellite Company Faulted Over Rocket Aid to China
1998-12-09.abstract: Secret Pentagon report concludes that Hughes Space and Communications, without proper authorization, gave China technological insights that are crucial to successful launchings of satellites and ballistic missiles; says Hughes scientists helped Chinese engineers in 1995 to improve sophisticated mathematical models necessary to predict effects of wind and other natural forces on rocket launching; says Chinese had been using 'oversimplified' mathematical analysis, resulting in failed satellite launchings; concludes that Hughes provided 'defense service' to China that violated US standards against helping Beijing make better satellites and missiles and required State Dept review; Hughes says that its actions were approved at time by Commerce Dept (M)

1998-12-10.title: Dissident From Shanghai Arrives in New York
1998-12-10.abstract: Yao Zhenxian, Chinese dissident fleeing new round of arrests of democracy activists in Shanghai, arrives in New York City and announces that he and other opponents of Chinese Government plan demonstration at United Nations on Dec 10 to protest crackdown (M)

1998-12-10.title: Clinton to Focus on Genocide and Child Labor Issues on Rights Day
1998-12-10.abstract: Pres Clinton will commemorate Human Rights Day by announcing creation of early-warning center to focus US intelligence resources on uncovering potential genocide and large increase in funding for programs to stop child labor around world (M)

1998-12-10.title: Spanish Judge Draws Up His Indictment
1998-12-10.abstract: Spanish Judge Baltasar Garzon, who issued arrest warrant for Gen Augusto Pinochet, will complete criminal indictment against him and send it to London (S)

1998-12-10.title: Ruling Party In Iraq Bars Inspectors From Offices
1998-12-10.abstract: Pres Saddam Hussein's Government bars inspectors from Baghdad city headquarters of his ruling Baath Party, barely two days into crucial round of surprise arms inspections in Iraq (M)

1998-12-10.title: Omagh Journal; After the Terror, the Epilogue Is Horror and Loss
1998-12-10.abstract: Article on Omagh, Northern Ireland, notes that it is still suffering after-effects of car bombing by Irish Republican Army splinter group that killed 29 people and wounded about 400 in Aug; map; photo (M)

1998-12-11.title: Iraq Again Hindering Inspections, U.N. Told
1998-12-11.abstract: Richard Butler, chief UN arms inspector for Iraq, tells Security Council that Iraqis have been interfering with inspections at number of sites; Baghdad toughens its stand against allowing entry to ruling party's offices, in contravention of Council resolutions; Butler says Iraqis have refused to explain why and where equipment and microorganisms thought to be associated with biological weapons program had been moved during break in inspections last month; photo (M)

1998-12-11.title: World Briefing
1998-12-11.abstract: EUROPE    SERBIA: EXAMINATION OF GRAVES IS BLOCKED -- A team of 19 Finnish forensic specialists investigating atrocities in Kosovo said the Serbian police barred them from opening a mass grave thought to contain the bodies of ethnic Albanians. Troops with armored vehicles blocked the Finns as they headed to the first of six sites they plan to search for bodies of civilians slaughtered in the conflict between Serbs and ethnic Albanian separatists in the province, in southern Serbia.    (AP)

1998-12-11.title: Russia Offers 1999 Budget: 'Honest' or 'Unrealistic'?
1998-12-11.abstract: Russian Govt finally unveils its 1999 spending plan to halt Russia's economic tumble; Yevgeny M Primakov calls economic blueprint, his first as Prime Min, a bold effort to boost Russia's hard-pressed factories and farms while clamping down on wasteful spending; 1999 budget is criticized as political exercise to attract Western financial aid while placating powerful constituencies at home; from tax revenues to inflation, budget is based on highly optimistic assumptions, which are unlikely to survive their bruising contact with Russia's dour economic realities; photo (M)

1998-12-11.title: Iranian Denies Seeking Biological Arms in Russia
1998-12-11.abstract: Iranian scientist, Mehdi Rezayat, denies that he had tried to recruit biologists who formerly worked on Moscow's germ-warfare program to develop similar weapons for Iran, as American officials and Russian scientists have reported; Rezayat was identified by New York Times on Dec 8 as a key figure in an Iranian purchasing network meant to speed development of nuclear, chemical and biological weapons (S)

1998-12-11.title: Judge Lays Out Pinochet Case In Full Detail
1998-12-11.abstract: Spanish judge indicts Gen Augusto Pinochet 'for the crimes of genocide, terrorism and torture' during his 17-year dictatorship in Chile, key step toward his trial in Madrid if he is eventually extradited from Britain (S)

1998-12-12.title: Soon, a Common Currency, But No European Consensus
1998-12-12.abstract: Leaders of supposedly united Europe, at summit meeting in Vienna, squabble about how much expansion will cost; argument is about money and efficiency of procedures assuring vetoes of original three small members of 1957 European Economic community--Belgium, Luxembourg, Netherlands--over decisions by original three large ones--Germany, France and Italy; another large issue is fat agricultural subsidies designed when France was weak agricultural economy and Germany was powerful industrial one, formula that would lead to political paralysis and financial chaos unless it is changed; photo (M)

1998-12-12.title: House Panel Votes to Impeach Clinton for Perjury : In Late Appeal, President Says, 'I Am Profoundly Sorry'
Sir Otto Frankel, Geneticist, 98; Warned of Extinction of Species
1998-12-12.abstract: Sir Otto Frankel, plant geneticist who was among first to warn of threat to whole species, dies at 98; photo (S)

1998-12-12.title: Pressure Builds on Brazil to Reassure Investors
1998-12-12.abstract: Brazil is under immense pressure to convince investors it can streamline its Government to reduce its dependence on outside financing; Asian collapse last year prompted total cooperation by local legislators, but now Government of Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso faces tough fight to push through unpopular economic measures; there is $41.5 billion bailout from International Monetary Fund in the offing; photo (M)

1998-12-12.title: At the U.N., Nations Are Urged to Prepare for 'Millennium Bug'
1998-12-12.abstract: Delegates from more than 120 countries attend first global convention of government represenatives to deal with so-called Millennium bug, held at United Nations; some of countries represented have not yet set up national projects to deal with problem; some last-minute attendees, notably France, express skepticism about extent of threat; common assumption is that most of world is far behind United States in preparing for 2000; Garter Group, consulting firm, projects that 30 countries, including Russia and China, are in highest-risk category, and that two-thirds or more of all businesses and government agencies in those countries will run into at least one major problem (M)

1998-12-13.title: Le Pen Feud With Key Deputy Has French Right in Upheaval
1998-12-13.abstract: France's National Front is in upheaval after Jean-Marie Le Pen, founder, expels Bruno Megret, the man who was behind party's drive for right-wing respectability; rift follows months of subterranean dissension (M)

1998-12-13.title: Manila Leader, Ally of Poor, Now Courts the Rich
1998-12-13.abstract: Joseph E Estrada, who successfully campaigned for President of Philippines as a champion of the poor, is accused of running an administration that does more for the nation's rich; critics say his relaxed style of leadership is allowing country to slip back into the corruption and cronyism that were hallmarks of Marcos regime (M)

1998-12-13.title: U.S. Hardens Stance on Yugoslav Leader
1998-12-13.abstract: US moves to undermine Pres Slobodan Milosevic's tight control over Yugoslavia; his grip has weakened because of his mishandling of rebellion in Serbian province of Kosovo; US now openly supports democratic Government in Montenegro, a part of Yugoslavia that Milosevic has hinted he might move against (M);

1998-12-13.title: Stiffer Rules Are a Threat To Refugees, Agency Says
1998-12-13.abstract: Dennis McNamara, a senior United Nations official in charge of protecting refugees, says tougher qualifications for asylum in both rich and poor countries are threatening the lives and safety of displaced people almost everywhere; singles out US as a major problem, citing severe new restrictions on asylum seekers and refugees trying to enter US (M)

1998-12-13.title: There's No Bossing a Democracy, Arafat Learns
1998-12-13.abstract: Palestinian Legislative Council, Palestinian Supreme Court and other fledgling institutions of democracy are increasingly frustrated with Yasir Arafat's viselike grip on reins of power in Palestinian Authority; still, experts say Pelestinians have moved more quickly and closer toward developing authentic democracy than any of their Arab neighbors, thanks largely to their relatively sizable intellectual elite, international help and the model of Israel; photo; map (M)

1998-12-14.title: Indispensable Man
1998-12-14.abstract: News analysis of Pres Pres Clinton visit to Israel and West Bank; even though Clinton suffered lukewarm greeting from Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu and his effort to save Wye peace agreement faces uncertain success, there is little question that he is engaged in critical mission and has made himself indispensable authority figure in blood feud between Israelis and Palestinians; photo (M)

1998-12-14.title: In Jerusalem, Clinton Visits A Fellow Embattled Leader
1998-12-14.abstract: Pres Clinton, forced to confront issue of impeachment, reluctantly interrupts efforts to rescue Middle East peace plan during visit to Jerusalem by addressing vote in Congress he says he cannot control; tells news conference his deposition in Paula Corbin Jones's sexual misconduct case was 'difficult and ambiguous and unhelpful,' but expresses none of recent remorse about affair with former intern, Monica S Lewinsky; says coming Congressional vote on impeachment is out of his hands; reportedly makes calls to two Democratic members of House Judiciary Committee to assess situation; makes most forthright appeal to members of House to consider censure as alternative; says approximately three-quarters of American people believe it is right thing to do; dismisses calls by Republican moderates who might support censure for admission that he lied under oath; both he and Israeli Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu seem far apart and peace initiative is in doubt as each try to grapple with complex interplay of domestic and foreign concerns; Clinton receives pugnacious reception from Netanyahu, who repeatedly reminds him at state dinner of Israel's fears and determination; lectures Clinton on Jews' lonely and turbulent history in presence of Israeli leaders whose support his Govt needs; reminds Clinton of ancient Jewish ties to Gaza and Bethlehem, Palestinian-controlled areas that Clinton will visit in next two days; Clinton repeatedly affirms American commitment to Israeli security, but also promotes Wye agreement, which calls for Israel to withdraw in phases from additional 13 percent of West Bank in exchange for Palestinian steps against terrorism; joins Pres Ezer Weizman in lighting first candle of Hanukkah; places oval stone, reportedly taken from Wye River, on tombstone of late Prime Min Yitzhak Rabin; photo (M)

1998-12-14.title: Outlook Is Bleak in Japan
1998-12-14.abstract: Bank of Japan reports business confidence is at lowest level in four and a half years (S)

1998-12-14.title: Soweto Journal; That Little White Ball Is Suddenly Colorblind
1998-12-14.abstract: Refurbishment of golf course at Soweto Country Club has become symbol of renewal in South Africa's most famous black township; club, which many say was built to create buffer zone between black and mixed-race areas during era of apartheid, is trying to attract new members; its efforts described; photos (M)

1998-12-14.title: Seoul and U.S. At Odds Over Joint Battalion
1998-12-14.abstract: SEOUL—  The mysterious death of a South Korean Army officer has touched off a debate here between U.S. and South Korean forces over who should command a joint battalion at the critical crossing between South and North Korea.

1998-12-15.title: World Briefing
1998-12-15.abstract: EUROPE  BRITAIN: CHALLENGE BY PINOCHET  Lawyers for Gen. Augusto Pinochet said they would turn to England's High Court to challenge the approval last week by the Home Secretary, Jack Straw, of the Spanish petition to extradite their client to Madrid to face charges of crimes against humanity. In another legal move, the lawyers will also go to the House of Lords to seek a review of its decision denying the former Chilean dictator immunity from arrest.    Warren Hoge (NYT)

1998-12-15.title: London and Dublin Tackle Dual Threats to North's Pact
1998-12-15.abstract: British and Irish Governments move to deal with rising tension between Protestant majority and Roman Catholic minority in Northern Ireland; hostility stems from lagghing peace effort and from old dispute about Protestant parade through Catholic area; police fear new violence (S)

1998-12-15.title: China to Try 2 Dissidents on Thursday but Denies Them Lawyers
1998-12-15.abstract: Subversion trial of Qin Yongmin and Wang Youcai, prominent dissidents trying to promote new political party in China, will be held on December 17; relatives say both have been denied access to legal counsel (M)

1998-12-15.title: North Korea Said to Drop Demand on Atom Inspection
1998-12-15.abstract: North Korea reportedly drops demand that US pay hundreds of millions of dollars for right to inspect huge underground center American intelligence agencies fear could be used to make nuclear weapons; reportedly suggest they would be willing to allow US to inspect center in exchange for food to ease famine that has plagued country for years (M)

1998-12-15.title: TARNISHED LAUREATE: A special report.; Nobel Winner Finds Her Story Challenged
1998-12-15.abstract: Book by American anthropologist David Stoll, Rigoberta Menchu and the Story of All Poor Guatemalans, casts doubt on accuracy of her 1992 autobiography--I, Rigoberta Menchu--for which she received Nobel Peace Prize and became overnight celebrity and much-sought-after crusader for human rights, special report, Tarnished Laureate; autobiography, first published in Spanish in 1983 at height of Guatemala's brutal civil war, tells wrenching tale of violence, destruction, misery and exploitation; Stoll, after decade of interviews with more than 120 people and archival research, concludes that key details of her story are untrue; says book is not eyewitness account since Menchu repeatedly describes experiences she never had herself; his assertions are corroborated by New York Times in interviews with members of Menchu's family, as well as with friends, neighbors and former classmates that indicate many of book's main episodes are either totally fabricated or seriously exaggerated; Menchu defends book as part of historical memory and patrimony of Guatemala, dismissing criticisms as part of racist political agenda to gain attention and publicity; photos; map (L)

1998-12-16.title: Justice Dept. Warns Group Aiding Iraq That It Needs a Permit
1998-12-16.abstract: American group opposed to sanctions against Iraq runs into trouble with Federal Government for taking medicines and toys to Baghdad to give to Iraqis who are suffering from effects of embargo; group, Voices in the Wilderness, plans to announce at news conf in Chicago that it and four members had received warning from Office of Foreign Assets Control in Justice Dept that orgn might be fined more than $160,000 for taking goods to Iraq without authorization in 1996 and last year (M)

1998-12-16.title: Aid Nations Asking Reforms By Bosnians
1998-12-16.abstract: United States and other countries providing support to carrying out Bosnian peace accord meet in Madrid and agree that they should press for deeper economic and governmental reforms to prepare nation to survive on its own (S)

1998-12-16.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1998-12-16.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: START TREATY BACKED -- The commander of the strategic missile force, Gen. Vladimir Yakovlev, backed ratification of the Start II arms-reduction treaty with the United States, saying Russia's nuclear weapons will soon reach the end of their service life. The United States Senate ratified the treaty in 1996. But the Communist-led opposition in the Russian Parliament has blocked ratification, contending that dismantling the weapons would harm security and be too expensive.   (AP)    RUSSIA: STATE OF EMERGENCY IN CHECHNYA -- The Chechnya Parliament declared a one-month state of emergency to combat a wave of crime in the breakaway republic. Four Western hostages were beheaded there last week. On Monday the Parliament refused to let President Aslan Maskhadov call up army reservists to pursue criminal gangs.   (AP)

1998-12-16.title: Tie to NATO Forces Ireland To Reconsider Neutral Role
1998-12-16.abstract: Irish Government is moving cautiously but unmistakably toward a relaxation of Ireland's bedrock national policy: military neutrality it has proudly maintained since gaining independence from Britain 76 years ago; change is being pressed by Foreign Min David Andrews, whose governing political party, Fianna Fail, has always denounced any move away from strict neutrality as disloyal to Ireland, if not treasonous; now Andrews is arguing that Ireland's neutrality should be considered to permit country to join so-called Partnership for Peace, American-sponsored group of nations that do not belong to NATO but have agreed to coordinate military policies with it (M)

1998-12-17.title: 2 Rival Dramas, Inevitably Entwined
1998-12-17.abstract: Critic's Notebook column on television coverage of start of US air strikes against Iraq on December 16 (M)

1998-12-17.title: No Reservoir Of Credibility
1998-12-17.abstract: News analysis of Pres Clinton's decision to launch air strikes against Iraq; reaction among politicians has pushed him closer to reality that long months of evasions and legalisms and his enemies' unceasing denunciations of him as liar have been costly to both him and nation; his credibility is crumbling, especially but not exclusively among Republicans on Capitol Hill; some sober and experienced leaders no longer accept his word (M)

1998-12-17.title: A New Scandal Rocks Ireland, Provoking Turmoil in Parliament
1998-12-17.abstract: New scandal emerges in Ireland over assertions of corruption and nepotism at highest level of Government, plunging country into national debate; debate comes after disclosure of official ruling that former Prime Min Charles J Haughey owes no taxes on $2 million gift from businessman, Ben Dunne, and that decision was made by Ronan Kelly, tax official who is brother-in-law of present Prime Min Bertie Ahern, whose political career was fostered by Haughey (M)

1998-12-17.title: Details Given, and 5 More Charged, in Tanzania Bombing
1998-12-17.abstract: Federal prosecutors, in first detailed account of August 7 bombing of US Embassy in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, say it was carried out by group of men who detonated bomb packed with car batteries and oxygen tanks, apparently to enhance lethal force of explosion; say explosion, which killed 11 people including one bomber, and wounded 80 others, was photographed by one suspect who later fled; identifies five men as followers of Saudi exile Osama bin Laden, who is suspected of organizing simultaneous attacks on embassies in Tanzania and Kenya as well as other acts of terrorism against Americans abroad; listed as Fahad Mohammed Ali Msalam, Ahmed Khalfan Ghailani, Khalfan Khamis Mohammed, Sheik Ahmed Salim Swedan, Mustafa Mohammed Fadhil; their photos (M)

1998-12-17.title: A Fugitive Zimbabwean Leader Surrenders
1998-12-17.abstract: Zimbabwe's former President, Rev Canaan Banana, who fled Zimbabwe before conviction on sodomy and sexual assault charges, surrenders to authorities in capital of Harare (S)

1998-12-18.title: Indonesia's Leader Warns of 'Disintegration' From Strife
1998-12-18.abstract: Student demonstrators clash with soldiers in Jakarta as Indonesian Pres B J Habibie warns that continued protests could lead to 'disintegration of the nation'; scores of students are treated at hospitals after about 4,000 demonstrators, some of them throwing gasoline bombs, challenge security forces guarding Parliament building; photo (M)

1998-12-18.title: JETS SAID TO AVOID POISON GAS SITES
1998-12-18.abstract: Pentagon officials say US and British forces are taking care to avoid hitting Iraqi factories suspected of producing chemical and biological weapons; say that damage at military, intelligence and communications targets has been extensive; say strikes avoided plants out of fear of unleashing plumes of poisons and killing civilians; say attack, which represents Pres Clinton's biggest military operation, appeared to surprise Iraqis, who had done little to disperse troops or weapons; Defense Sec William S Cohen and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chmn Henry H Shelton outline mission intended to weaken Saddam Hussein's Government and military, including missile sites and bases central to launching chemical or biological weapons; map; photo (M)

1998-12-18.title: Limerick (Stab City, No Less) Rebounds
1998-12-18.abstract: Limerick, Ireland, 1,000-year-old city of 52,000 people, is seen losing its inferiority complex; few years ago, third-largest Irish city was known in rest of country as a bad place to visit, but you wouldn't want to live there, either; city has changed for the better, as Ireland's economy has boomed (M)

1998-12-18.title: U.S. Journalist Slain in Mexico, Autopsy Shows
1998-12-18.abstract: Mexican forensic examiners say that American reporter Philip True, who disappeared while hiking through rugged mountains in western Mexico, was killed by being hit over the head and choked; earlier reports say he had died in accidental fall; Pres Ernesto Zedillo orders Federal and state authorities to spare no resources in investigating slaying; True, 50, was Mexico-based correspondent for San Antonio Express-News; had been a solo hiker since he was teen-ager (M)

1998-12-18.title: U.S. Closes 38 Embassies In Africa as a Precaution
1998-12-18.abstract: US closes 38 embassies in Africa for at least two days to protect employees against possible terrorist reprisals for US air strikes on Iraq; State Dept officials say embassies in South Africa and Nigeria will remain open (M)

1998-12-19.title: World Briefing
1998-12-19.abstract: EUROPE    POLAND: OPENING SECRET FILES Polish legislators, overriding the veto of President Alexander Kwasniewski, pushed through a bill opening access to the files of the Communist-era secret police. The bill will allow Poles who felt they were the victims of Communist repression to see personal files compiled by the authorities before 1989 and determine if they were discriminated against and possibly who informed against them.   (Reuters)    GERMANY: A BREAK FOR DESERTERS Germany gave World War II deserters and their families another year to apply for a onetime payment of $4,500. Parliament offered compensation in May 1997 to men convicted by the Nazis of fleeing Hitler's army, after years of debate about whether they were heroes or traitors. Since then, 2,124 people have applied.   (AP)

1998-12-19.title: Pentagon Is Sticking To Timetable And Targets
1998-12-19.abstract: Pentagon has limited its attack on Iraq to sharp, swift assault lasting no more than few days, with no intention of even seriously crippling Pres Saddam Hussein; finite list of targets was drawn up with eye toward avoiding American casualties, and creating such vague and limited benchmark of success that it would be fairly easy to declare mission achieved; some Republicans in Congress have called on Clinton Administration to do more to punish Iraq and oust Hussein, but few support what that would take: prolonged commitment of American forces, including ground troops; map; charts (M)

1998-12-19.title: Both Sides in Ulster Carve Out Crucial Accord on Cabinet
1998-12-19.abstract: For first time since Ireland was partitioned in 1922, leaders of Northern Ireland's Protestant majority and Catholic minority agree to specific reforms that will give Catholics more power in British province; breakthrough after eight months of stalemate allows both sides to set up 10-member ruling Executive Cabinet that would include two representatives of Sinn Fein; negotiators sidestep issue of disarming Irish Republican Army and other guerrillas, problem that has blocked peace effort since general agreement was reached in April; photo (M)

1998-12-19.title: Memo From Moscow; Impeachment Over Sex? Try Genocide or Treason
1998-12-19.abstract: Russian Parliament is trying to impeach Pres Boris N Yeltsin over accusations of genocide, treason and abetting murder, and almost no one gives it second thought; most people expect proceedings to continue past time when Yeltsin ends his second term and leaves office in 2000; Communist Party and its allies on far right inspired and have managed impeachment from its start in May; photo (M)

1998-12-19.title: U.S. Reports Mixed Results on 89 Targets in Iraq
1998-12-19.abstract: Defense Department says it is able to confirm that only 18 of 89 targets were destroyed or severely damaged on third day of air assault on Iraq, despite waves of bombers and more laser-guided cruise missiles than were used during 1991 Persian Gulf war; Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman Gen Henry H Shelton acknowledges that results of US and British air campaign have been mixed so far; Pres Saddam Hussein remains defiant; calls attackers 'agents of Satan' in first televised address since start of bombing campaign; US military planes drop thousands of leaflets warning Iraqi ground troops they face bombing raids if they try to mobilize (M)

1998-12-20.title: Israel Battles New Foreign Foe: Music
1998-12-20.abstract: Israeli parliament tries to counter wide influence of American popular culture with bill to require that half of songs on national radio stations be in Hebrew; Israeli music itself is melange of East and West, with heavy Middle Eastern and Mediterranean influences that mix with rock; photo; critics see hopeless attempt to legislate tastes (S)

1998-12-20.title: Iraqis Are Called Masters of Concealment
1998-12-20.abstract: UN weapons inspectors and other arms experts say Iraqis are masters at hiding weaponry, and have extremely well-developed plans for moving them out of way of bombs; Iraqis reportedly have also fine-tuned ability to reconstitute programs quickly; Scott Ritter sys UN inspectors have seen emergency dispersal plan; photo of Iraqi airfield, showing planes that were removed from hangars before allied bombing (S)

1998-12-20.title: Panel Finds French Errors In Judgment On Rwanda
1998-12-20.abstract: French parliamentary committee on French military role in Rwanda concludes that succesive Governments made 'errors of judgment,' but absolves France of any responsibility for mass killings in 1994; French press charged that military advisers helped train Rwandan forces that carried out slaughters; committee criticizes UN for delaying military action, and pins blame for that on international community, led by US (S)

1998-12-20.title: Ex-Soviets Are Focus Of Inquiry By the Swiss
1998-12-20.abstract: Switzerland is concerned that rich free-spending Russians who have appeared since fall of Communism are using country as haven where they can stow ill-gotten cash from former Soviet Union; authorities recently detained former Ukrainian prime minister, Pavlo Lazarenko, who entered on Panamanian passport, and indicted him for money-laundering; Geneva prosecutors also brought organized-crime charges against Sergei Mikhailov, reputed Moscow mob kingpin who had been living quietly in Switzerland; Mikhailov was acquitted for insufficient evidence, thwarting effort to send message to Russian gangsters; Russia and Ukraine have both tried to seek out illegal funds deposited in Switzerland (M)

1998-12-20.title: Nova Scotia Ex-Premier Is Cleared of Charges of Rape in 1950's
1998-12-20.abstract: Gerald Regan, former premier of Nova Scotia, is aquitted of rape and attempted rape charges related to incidents that allegedly took place forty years ago; three women testified that Regan, now 70, attacked them when they were teen-agers; court does not permit 19 other women to testify to alleged assaults or harassment (S)

1998-12-21.title: This Time, Little Damage Is to Be Seen in Baghdad
1998-12-21.abstract: Iraq's capital of Baghdad appears to have avoided heavy damage in American and British air raids that lasted for 70 hours; preliminary Western assessments of damage suggest nationwide attacks were effective, even though life in city seems to have been hardly disrupted; even if raids were successful in destroying Iraqi military bases and centers where chemical and biological weapons may have been developed, there is no sign that they came near to achieving West's larger political objective of removing Pres Saddam Hussein; Hussein has emerged at least as powerful as he was before military campaign; photo (M)

1998-12-21.title: Allahabad Journal; Caste and Campus: India's Ivy Is Overwhelmed
1998-12-21.abstract: Once-prestigious Allhabad University, alma mater of three former Indian Prime Ministers and formerly called Oxford of India, has lost its way and is mired in stagnation and decay; buildings are decrepit and campus built for 5,000 students now has 60,000; academic calender has so many postponed sessions that years can go by without required courses being offered, and long delays in grading exams are commonplace; university's decline, while extreme case, is symptomatic of system overstuffed with students and undernourished with resources; India has approximately 10,000 universities and colleges, double number of 20 years ago; while some remain first-rate, large portion of rest, especially in poorest 27 states, are overwhelmed creations of haphazard growth; map; photos (M)

1998-12-21.title: Zambia Announces Deal to Sell Copper Mines
1998-12-21.abstract: Deal to sell state-owned copper mines in Zambia is reached after two years of on-and-off negotiations; Anglo American Corporation of South Africa will take over important Nchanga, Nkana and Konkola mines (M)

1998-12-21.title: Top Dissident Gets 13 Years After Trial In Beijing
1998-12-21.abstract: Xu Wenli, top Chinese disident, receives 13-year sentence after three-hour trial in Beijing on charges of trying to overthrow state; previously spent 12 years in prison for peacefully advocating democratic change; reportedly condemns sentence as political persecution (M)

1998-12-21.title: What Now? Doubts About U.S. and U.N. Policy on Iraq Increase
1998-12-21.abstract: News analysis finds four days of American and British air strikes have only increased doubts over US and UN policy toward Iraq; Pres Saddam Hussein has emerged with position strengthened in Arab public opinion for having withstood high-tech US military might, and with capacity and scientists to produce biological and chemical weapons (M)

1998-12-22.title: U.S. Plans to Keep Up Pressure on Saddam : 'Cat and Mouse' Over
1998-12-22.abstract: PARIS—  With United Nations arms inspectors no longer able to function in Iraq, the Clinton administration intends to apply open-ended, direct military pressure on the Baghdad regime until the Iraqi leader, Saddam Hussein, finds a way to clear his country's record on clandestine weapons, a senior American official said Monday.

1998-12-22.title: 10 Years After Lockerbie, Still No Trial
1998-12-22.abstract: US tells Libya on 10th anniversary of bombing of Pan American jetliner over Lockerbie, Scotland, that it will face more sanctions if two Libyan suspects are not turned over for trial by Scottish judge in Netherlands by February 1999; Libyan leader Col Muammar el-Qaddafi apparently rejects compromise plan for trial in Netherlands, saying he wants international tribunal to hear case; photo (M)

1998-12-22.title: 3 Balloonists Press Flight, Then China Lifts Its Ban
1998-12-22.abstract: China belatedly grants balloonist Richard Branson, Steve Fossett and Per Lindstrand overflight permission, allowing them to continue round-the-world journey; permission comes after intense diplomatic efforts--including appeal by Prime Min Tony Blair; map (M)

1998-12-22.title: World Briefing
1998-12-22.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: MIXED RESULTS IN RUNOFF -- Runoff elections for the St. Petersburg legislative assembly, widely seen as a bellwether for democratic reformers, drew less than one-third of the eligible voters and ended with mixed results. Centrist and reform candidates took 23 of the 50 seats at stake. The Communist Party won 5, and independents and smaller factions the remaining 22. The leading reformist bloc, Yabloko, captured 8 of the 23 seats that it contested.   Michael Wines (NYT)

1998-12-22.title: Andre de Wavrin, 87, 'Col. Passy' of Resistance Fame, Dies
1998-12-22.abstract: Andre de Wavrin, the 'Col Passy' who forged French Resistance intelligence network during World War II; photo (M)

1998-12-23.title: Acteal Journal; Where Killings Defiled a Church, No Forgiveness
1998-12-23.abstract: Some 2,000 people gather at graves of 45 people, mostly women and children, massacred year ago in Acteal, Mexico, church; military helicopters interrupt re-enactment showing men in police uniforms taking part in massacre, deadliest single attack in Chiapas since Zapatista rebellion began; residents who fled small pro-Zapatista village in Chiapas return after six days in forest, but continue to fear attack from larger nearby pro-Government community; map; photos (M)

1998-12-23.title: Defining Goal in Iraq
1998-12-23.abstract: Pres Clinton and Congressional Republicans are deeply divided over how aggressively United States should pursue overthrow of Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein; Senate Intelligence Committee reportedly blocks Central Intelligence Agency plan to subvert Iraqi Government because key Republicans are not convinced Clinton is serious about ousting Hussein; Administration officials hold there is no quick way to oust him and admit they are not yet willing to undertake aggressive military and intelligence actions they say are needed to support any coup (M)

1998-12-23.title: Jordan Accepts Suharto Relative, Ex-General
1998-12-23.abstract: Jordan offers citizenship to Prabowo Subianto, former Indonesia general and ex-Pres Suharto's son-in-law, who has been living in Amman for four months; he refuses offer; eleven soldiers he once commanded go on trial on charges of abducting and torturing political dissidents (M)

1998-12-23.title: Iraqi Cultural Elite Pine for Old Days
1998-12-23.abstract: Iraq's one-time cultural elite suffer from crushing economic sanctions; several sophisticated professionals blame United States for destroying their way of life and shredding prospects of entire generation, interviews (M)

1998-12-23.title: Anger Rises Over Plans For Ontario
1998-12-23.abstract: Ontario's draft plan for development of vast tract of publicly owned northern woodlands stirs anger in Canada, where many urban residents cherish ideal of sparsely populated region as wilderness frontier and vacation area; plan balances increase in park lands with more diverse opportunies for industry, satisfying neither conservationists nor logging and mining industries (M)

1998-12-24.title: Balloonists Cross China And Leave The Mainland
1998-12-24.abstract: Three-man crew of ICO Global Challenge become first balloonists to cross Asian continent from one end to the other and the first to fly over towering Himalayan range; are relaxing over Yellow Sea after contronting series of crises; map (M)

1998-12-24.title: Guerrillas Fire Rockets Into Israel
1998-12-24.abstract: Muslim guerrillas in Lebanon fire rockets into Israel in retaliation for killing of a Lebanese woman and six of her children in an Israeli bombing raid on suspected guerrilla targets on Dec 22; 13 people are wounded; Party of God, or Hezbollah, claims responsibility; Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu says Israel will respond; photo; map (M)

1998-12-24.title: U.S.O. Lightens Life at Two Gulf Sites
1998-12-24.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen and United Services Organization roadshow visit Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia, aircraft carrier Enterprise in Persian Gulf and Army and Marine troops at Camp Doha in Kuwait; rock-and-roll show at Saudi site is killed out of fear of offending Saudi sensibilities during holy Islamic month of Ramadan; Ramadan, however, is not seen as obstacle to bombing of Iraq (M)

1998-12-24.title: The Misery Of Babylon In the Age Of Hussein
1998-12-24.abstract: Frustrations of modern Iraq are especially poignant in and around Babylon, whose ancient glory is invoked by Pres Saddam Hussein but whose people struggle under deprivations caused by eight years of economic sanctions; photo; map (L)

1998-12-24.title: Suharto Aide Denies He's a Jordan Citizen
1998-12-24.abstract: Prabowo Subianto, son-in-law of Indonesia's former president, Suharto, denies reports that he has been granted Jordanian citizenship; Government is investigating whether he was involved in abduction and torture of dissidents or instigation of riots that claimed 1,200 lives in May (S)

1998-12-25.title: French Far-Right Party Likely to Split as Le Pen Expels 7 Aides
1998-12-25.abstract: Jean-Marie Le Pen, founder of France's far-right National Front party, expels his former deputy, Bruno Megret, and six other party leader from party's executive body in clash over strategy for European Parliament elections; one of expelled members says it now seems likely two separate National Front slates of candidates would run in elections, with Megret and Le Pen running at top of two separate slates, both calling themselves 'National Front' (M)

1998-12-25.title: Emilio F. Mignone, 76, Dies; Argentine Rights Campaigner
1998-12-25.abstract: Emilio Fermin Mignone, lawyer who became Argentina's best-known campaigner for human rights, dies at age 76; photo; Mignone's daughter Monica, who worked in slums with Catholic volunteers, was arrested by armed men at family home in 1976 and was never seen again, one of thousands of Argentinians who disappeared during military's 'dirty war' (S)

1998-12-25.title: Bin Laden Denies Role in Embassy Bombings
1998-12-25.abstract: Osama bin Laden reportedly tells Taliban rulers of Afghanistan that he had no role in deadly bombings of two American embassies in Africa, but he does not regret what happened; bin Laden says he knows and respects some of men suspected in Aug 7 bombings in Kenya and Tanzania; Federal authorities have indicted at least 14 people, including bin Laden, in their investigation of embassy bombings and what investigators have called global terrorist organization; United States intelligence authorities consider bin Laden one of world's most dangerous terrorists (M)

1998-12-25.title: Attack by Serbs Shatters a Cease-Fire in Kosovo
1998-12-25.abstract: Serbian forces attack separatist guerrillas in northern Kosovo Province, ripping apart tenuous two-month cease-fire; leaders of international monitoring mission question whether it is pointless--and too dangerous--to stay; say two sides do not intend to solve their differences peacefully; diplomats say this latest attack is worst violation since cease-fire went into effect, and follows 11 days of combat and assassinations; photo; map (M)

1998-12-25.title: Kisangani Journal; Where War Is Forever, the Diamonds Are Cheap
1998-12-25.abstract: Rebellion in Congo has been hard on diamond businesses in Kisangani; dealers say prices used to be fairly uniform when telephones worked and they could call brokers in other cities or countries; now there is no way to get in touch with outside world; with lack of foreign buyers, dealers are stockpiling what they buy, waiting for latest war to be over; meanwhile, rebels are thought to be buying up diamonds, presumably to help finance war; photo; map (M)

1998-12-26.title: Tests Show High Mercury At Cambodia Dump Site
1998-12-26.abstract: Initial tests of tons of waste dumped outside Sihanoukville, Cambodia, by Formosa Plastics Corp of Taiwan indicate that mercury level is thousands of times higher than safety standards permit (S)

1998-12-26.title: Yeltsin Agrees To Closer Ties With Belarus
1998-12-26.abstract: Pres Boris Yeltsin of Russia and Pres Aleksandr G Lukashenko of Belarus say they intend to begin unifying their currencies and much of their economies in 1999, an action that would stop just short of a political merger that would again extend Russian territory to the border of Poland; announcement is victory for Lukashenko, who says he hopes that two countries will enter millennium as one; Russian officials are cooler to idea; American officials have quietly opposed attempts at greater unity for fear that i will destabilize young democracies in eastern Europe (M)

1998-12-26.title: North Korea Warns U.S. That It Can Launch Another Missile
1998-12-26.abstract: North Korea warns US that it is prepared to launch another medium-range missile; Clinton Administration has warned the North that such a step would jeopardize international aid it was promised in exchange for freezing its nuclear weapons program (M)

1998-12-26.title: A Search For Identity At Impasse In Canada
1998-12-26.abstract: Police in Canada and US are unable to identify a young found naked and dazed in Montreal parking lot on Oct 12; he has no idea who he is or how he got there; is given hypnosis and injection of 'truth serum,' after which he says he might be James Edward Brighton from Morristown, NJ; photo (M)

1998-12-26.title: South Africa Is a Magnet For Weather With Drama
1998-12-26.abstract: Lightning and tornadoes harass South Africa, whose weather is generally benign; one tornado nearly gets Pres Nelson Mandela (M)

1998-12-27.title: Belarus's Chief Pursues Dream To Revive the Old Soviet Union
1998-12-27.abstract: Pres Aleksandr G Lukashenko pursues his dream of making Belarus a model socialist state and merging it into Russia, thus beginning the rebuilding of former Soviet empire, which he might have a good chance of leading; such a union would be hard enough to achieve, given that Russia is moving in capitalist direction while Belarus is entrenched in old-style socialism, and harder still if Belarus's stumbling economy should ground to a complete halt, saddling Russia with the cost of maintaining another 10 million impoverished people; Minsk in 1998 resembles Moscow in late 1980's; photo; map (M)

1998-12-27.title: An Ugly America, Iraqis Say; No Reflection on Americans
1998-12-27.abstract: Iraqi people are remarkably friendly to American visitors despite American air strikes and deterioration of their lives under economic sanctions; the animosity they bear toward US Government is increasingly focused on Pres Clinton, who is for them the personification of forces that are bombing and blockading them (M)

1998-12-27.title: Canadians Riled at Plan To Cut Ties To Queen
1998-12-27.abstract: Informal Government proposal for severing Canada's ties to Queen Elizabeth II is widely assailed, and its backers are now backpeddling (M)

1998-12-27.title: Faulty Condoms Thwart AIDS Fight in Africa
1998-12-27.abstract: Another article in Dead Zones series describes how efforts to contain AIDS epidemic in Africa, especially South Africa, have been undermined by prevalence of substandard condoms; most condoms are made in India, Malaysia, Thailand and China, and while some operate very scientifically, others turn out a high percentage of defective condoms that makers try to unload wherever they can; South Africa became a dumping ground because it is rich enough to buy its own condoms and used ineffective test regimen; has now adopted testing standards used by British, American and other big aid agencies; photo; map (M)

1998-12-27.title: A Crusader's Legacy Blurs in Brazil's Bitter Politics
1998-12-27.abstract: Much has changed for better in Amazon rain forest of Brazil in 10 years since murder of Francisco (Chico) Mendes, outspoken defender of rubber tappers and the forest; his Workers' Party has just won mayoralty of Xapuri and governorship of Acre, and party members fill state's two seats in Brazil's Senate; millions of acres of rain forest have been reserved for the rubber tappers and nut gatherers whom Mendes defended; however, tappers' lives are as wretched as ever because of decline of rubber prices; and Mendes's widow, Ilzamar Gadelha Mendes, is in bitter feud with his party; photo; map (M)

1998-12-28.title: Germ Weapons: In Soviet Past Or in the New Russia's Future?
1998-12-28.abstract: Belated discovery of research on new and deadlier generation of germ warfare weapons by Russian military officers in Kazakhstan just as Soviet Union was ending its confrontation with West in 1980's fuels fierce debate in Washington over whether military is heeding Pres Boris Yeltsin's 1992 order to abandon germ warfare; discovery of animal testing of highly contagious Marburg virus at remote laboratory complex near Stepnogorsk in final days of cold war has led Clinton Administration officials and Republicans in Congress to assert that Russia is still secretly researching germ weapons; US officials say while there is scant hard evidence to support suspicions, Moscow's reluctance to open up military bases is ominous sign; skeptics of Western aid programs that pay salaries to former Russian germ scientists to thwart their recruitment by rogue or terrorist states say Moscow's lies about germ warfare program in past make trust and cooperation impossible; note Yeltsin has banned Russian experts from discussing any aspect of country's germ history and has retained several generals instrumentsl in Soviet program; worry particularly that research may be quietly proceeding on some of least known Soviet advances, such as 'bio-regulators' or germ compounds capable of controlling human moods, heart rhythms and sleep patterns; supporters of Western programs say while Russia may not be trusted unconditionally, partnership is best chance for verifying Moscow's claim to have renounced germ weapons; photos; map (L)

1998-12-28.title: In Drive on Dissidents, China Gives 4th Severe Sentence in Week
1998-12-28.abstract: China sentences 42-year-old Zhang Shanguang to 10-year prison sentence for providing intelligence to hostile foreign organizations; charges related to interview about farmer protests with US-financed Radio Free Asia; Zhang, who is fourth dissenter to receive lengthy prison sentence in week, previously served seven years for organizing independent labor union in 1989 (M)

1998-12-28.title: World Briefing
1998-12-28.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: 10 NUCLEAR MISSILES DEBUT -- Russia deployed 10 new Topol-M nuclear missiles in the Saratov region, 450 miles southeast of Moscow. The deployment was a major step for Russia's cash-shy military, because the Government does not have enough money to maintain all its armed forces and decided to concentrate on developing the single-warhead Topol-M, which has a range of 6,200 miles.    (AP)

1998-12-28.title: Iraq a Pediatrician's Hell: No Way to Stop the Dying
1998-12-28.abstract: Iraq's medical system is all but paralyzed as result of economic sanctions imposed by UN in 1990; hardest hit are childrens' hospitals where patients are succumbing to easily curable ailments because of lack of drugs and equipment; Governmment spending on medicine and equipment has fallen by more than 90 percent since sanctions were imposed; no new hospitals have been built during period, even though population has grown from 15 million to 22 million; photo (M)

1998-12-28.title: Challenge for Jewish President/ Weighty Matters of Conscience : A Swiss 'Facilitator' Searches for the Middle
1998-12-28.abstract: BERN—  Ruth Dreifuss says it almost before her visitors are settled into their chairs: She will not be an alibi, a public relations prop depicting Switzerland as reconciled with its past, or a marker to paint its history with the harsh, dark lines of caricature.

1998-12-29.title: Schroder Aide Typifies New German Subtlety
1998-12-29.abstract: Article profiles Germany's Interior Min Otto Schily, who typifies new class that has come to power and is changing country's political culture; new breed of politicians has more checkered past and more nuanced allegiances; represent departure from scene of more predictable German--solid, solemn and unsurprising--which US and allies find disorienting; Schily comments; photo (M)

1998-12-29.title: Konya Journal; Festival of Rumi, Poet of Life's Dance
1998-12-29.abstract: Annual festival commemmorating Jalaluddin Rumi, Islamic mystic poet who conceived dervish dance as part of lifelong quest for religious rapture, is held in Konya, Turkey, which is world capital of Rumi cult; annual festival is city's main tourist attraction; map; photo (M)

1998-12-29.title: U.S. JETS ATTACK IRAQ MISSILE POST
1998-12-29.abstract: US warplanes patrolling northern Iraq attack Iraqi antiaircraft missile battery near Mosul after coming under fire themselves; Iraq says it repulsed attack and probably shot down enemy aircraft; says four soldiers died in attack; US Defense Dept dismisses claim; says all 27 American and British aircraft returned safely to bases in Incirlik, Turkey; latest incident comes two days after Iraq's warning that it would fire on any aircraft flying in 'no flight' zones US and allies created over northern and southern Iraq after Persian Gulf War ended in 1991; appears to mark Iraqi attempt to provoke new confrontation with US; photo; map (M)

1998-12-29.title: World Briefing
1998-12-29.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: WARNING ON PINOCHET CASE  Britain's Lord Chancellor -- the Government's chief law officer -- said the legal system risked being brought into disrepute by the Augusto Pinochet case. Lord Derry Irvine referred to the decision by the House of Lords, the highest court, to set aside its own ruling in November that the former Chilean dictator did not have immunity from prosecution because he was a former head of state.    (Reuters)

1998-12-29.title: Iraqi Chief Finds New Enemies, Even Among Former Friends
1998-12-29.abstract: Iraq's Pres Saddam Hussein complicates situation with UN Security Council with stream of bellicose statements against not only his enemies in London and Washington but also friends like Russia, France and his Arab neighbors; Iraq's future ties with UN remain in doubt as Govt vows it will never allow arms inspectors to return and is balking at further cooperation with agency, which administers huge relief program in country; had generally cooperated with aid workers, despite objections to presence of arms inspectors (M)

1998-12-30.title: Democrats' Hopes Fade for Pretrial Censure of Clinton in the Senate
1998-12-30.abstract: WASHINGTON—  President Bill Clinton's supporters in the Senate acknowledged Tuesday that they were making little headway with proposals for censure of the president — rather than conviction and banishment from office on articles of impeachment — and they worked with Republicans to settle details of a Senate trial next week that has become all but certain.

1998-12-30.title: Hamilton Journal; Of Stern Scots, Cheeky Kids and an Early Bedtime
